title: Repository Repair, Release, and Collaboration Capstone · Repository Trace: Release Audit
summary: Recover and ship a repository containing mixed work, a conflict, a mistaken commit, a remote update, and a release deadline. Planned artifact: Add and verify the git release audit increment in a complete Git foundations evidence portfolio. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Audit source identity, tree cleanliness, review range, checks, tag, artifact provenance, and remote references before release.
Coordinate rewritten history, conflicts, ownership, release changes, and recovery instructions with explicit affected refs and people.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# git-basics cumulative repository evidence notebook
# Git operations change only disposable simulated repository state.
# Inspect status, diffs, graph, refs, and recovery before every mutation.

steps.title: Predict Release Audit
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory repository incident combines mixed work, divergent refs, one conflict, an incorrect public commit, and release v3.0. Preserve recovery refs, repair the least destructive layer, and verify graph plus tree.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Release Audit contract for the supplied data.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for audit source identity, tree cleanliness, review range, checks, tag, artifact provenance, and remote references before release.

content.text: A theory repository incident combines mixed work, divergent refs, one conflict, an incorrect public commit, and release v3.0. Preserve recovery refs, repair the least destructive layer, and verify graph plus tree.

content.title: Commit before feedback
content.text: Choose from the stated data before opening the worked model. Record which Release Audit condition decided your prediction.

content.label: Authority to consult after committing
content.value: Pro Git, Second Edition — Controls cumulative conceptual sequence from setup through internals, collaboration, tooling, and distributed workflows.

hints: Name the Release Audit precondition or invariant that the theory case must preserve.

Trace this concrete case before choosing: A theory repository incident combines mixed work, divergent refs, one conflict, an incorrect public commit, and release v3.0. Preserve recovery refs, repair the least destructive layer, and verify graph plus tree.
Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: A green release pipeline proves the selected source commit was intended.

options.text: Audit source identity, tree cleanliness, review range, checks, tag, artifact provenance, and remote references before release. Apply it to the concrete input and state the observable result.
A green release pipeline proves the selected source commit was intended.
Apply the visible technique immediately, omit assumptions, and treat one successful output as complete proof.

steps.title: Bound Release Audit
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory repository incident combines mixed work, divergent refs, one conflict, an incorrect public commit, and release v4.0. Preserve recovery refs, repair the least destructive layer, and verify graph plus tree.

After tracing the worked code, choose the Release Audit application whose preconditions and limits still match the supplied case.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for audit source identity, tree cleanliness, review range, checks, tag, artifact provenance, and remote references before release.

content.text: Audit source identity, tree cleanliness, review range, checks, tag, artifact provenance, and remote references before release. Use this as an observable contract: name its preconditions, trace the state or value change, and state what evidence would disprove the result.

content.title: Reasoning move
content.text: Locate repository and operation state, compare working tree, index, refs, and graph, preserve a recovery reference, preview the smallest change, then verify both tree behavior and history topology.

content.title: Worked case pattern
content.text: A worked example repository incident combines mixed work, divergent refs, one conflict, an incorrect public commit, and release v3.0. Preserve recovery refs, repair the least destructive layer, and verify graph plus tree. A defensible solution applies Release Audit, preserves the governing invariant or value contract, and connects the observed result to a complete Git foundations evidence portfolio.

content.code:
# Evidence: changed Git case 2
# hypothesis: the mismatch is isolated to one repository layer
git status --short --branch
git diff
git diff --staged
git log --graph --decorate --oneline -n 6
# observation: name worktree, index, ref, and graph evidence separately
content.caption: Runnable repository-state trace example; predict its result before running, then change one boundary input.

content.title: Non-worked case
content.text: A green release pipeline proves the selected source commit was intended. This fails the Release Audit contract because it omits a precondition, invariant, ownership rule, or counterexample needed by this exact case.

content.value: Pro Git, Second Edition (current web edition 2026-07) — Controls cumulative conceptual sequence from setup through internals, collaboration, tooling, and distributed workflows.

steps.title: Sequence Release Audit evidence
steps.instruction: Order the Release Audit evidence cycle for this theory task before implementing it.
steps.why: Correct order keeps Release Audit assumptions visible before execution and verifies its result against a boundary case afterward.

steps.title: Build Release Audit evidence
steps.instruction: Append a safe repository investigation headed "# Evidence: git-release-audit-1gr5gj" with a concrete hypothesis, one bounded simulator command, and an observation that changes the next decision.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns Release Audit into observable performance evidence.

content.code:

# hypothesis: [replace with one falsifiable cause]
# command: choose one bounded simulator observation
# observation: [replace with the result that changes the next decision]

steps.title: Repair the Release Audit defect
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory repository incident combines mixed work, divergent refs, one conflict, an incorrect public commit, and release v3.0. Preserve recovery refs, repair the least destructive layer, and verify graph plus tree.

A teammate acts on this belief: “A green release pipeline proves the selected source commit was intended.” Which repair addresses the cause?

stimulus.title: Release Audit failure evidence
stimulus.caption: A plausible visible result conflicts with the governing contract and requires a cause-level repair.

lines.label: observed
lines.text: A green release pipeline proves the selected source commit was intended.

lines.label: required
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Audit source identity, tree cleanliness, review range, checks, tag, artifact provenance, and remote references before release.

options.text: Apply the visible technique immediately, omit assumptions, and treat one successful output as complete proof.
Reconstruct the conditions for Release Audit, gather a counterexample, repair the artifact, and add a regression case.
Keep the artifact unchanged and rewrite the explanation so the current output appears intentional.

steps.title: Defend the Release Audit model
steps.instruction: Write the theory evidence note for Release Audit: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A theory repository incident combines mixed work, divergent refs, one conflict, an incorrect public commit, and release v4.0. Preserve recovery refs, repair the least destructive layer, and verify graph plus tree.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Audit source identity, tree cleanliness, review range, checks, tag, artifact provenance, and remote references before release.
